This proposal is destined exclusively to HYGEN ENERGY HOLDINGS LIMITED

(“HYGEN”) and is communicated for the sole purpose of allowing HYGEN to evaluate the opportunity of entering into a contract with BEHS and, as the case may be, such further purpose as shall be expressly allowed by such contract, and may not be reproduced, used or disclosed, in whole or in part, nor used for any other purpose.

The Bradford Low Carbon Hydrogen project is a joint venture between Hygen Energy Holdings Ltd and N-Gen Energy Solutions Ltd sister company to Northern Gas Networks formed to develop and operate an advanced green hydrogen generation plant and refuelling station on NGN’s former gas holder site at Peace Street, Bradford, BD4 8UF. The facility is designed to deliver up to 35 MWe of electrolytic hydrogen production, with a nameplate capacity of approximately 630 kg/h (≈ 15 000 kg/day), primarily to fill tube trailers and bowsers for field refuelling of construction equipment.
Image 1.1 Layout for the Bradford Low Carbon Hydrogen Project

Broaden Energy is committed to executing the design, supply, installation, and commissioning of Hydrogen Production System consisting of electrolysers, compressors,storage and required utilities for The Bradford Low Carbon Hydrogen project. This project will be executed in strict accordance with the project specifications, ensuring integration with the existing site situation while adhering to Client's standards and regulations. Below is a breakdown of our proposed scope and responsibilities:

1. Supply and Installation of Equipment: All equipment, included in Part A, B and C as proposed and supporting systems, will be designed, manufactured, and delivered as per the technical specifications and Bill of Quantities (BOQ) in section 2.1.1. The equipment installation covers supervision of mechanical, electrical, and plumbing (MEP) as well as gas installations limited to supervision within the battery limit of our proposal. These activities will be performed under the support of the client or nominated EPC in line with the responsibility matrix under this chapter, ensuring compliance with installation protocols by Broaden. We will also facilitate Factory Acceptance Testing (FAT) at Broaden Energy’s facility prior to shipment and Client representatives' attendance to ensure quality assurance. 2. Compliance and Supervision: During installation, all work will be closely supervised and inspected by BEHS personnel to guarantee alignment with project and system requirements. 3. Safety Systems and Documentation: Safety is paramount in the design and operation of any Hydrogen Plant. We design and install all necessary safety systems in all our products these include but are not limited to, safety instrumented systems (SIS), hydrogen leak detection, and emergency shutdown systems integrated in the PLC. All supplied documentation will outline the specifics of owning and operating a Hydrogen Generation System. Broaden Energy will submit the documentation, including P&ID, PFD, BFD, Heat and Mass Balance schematics, Cause & Effect matrix, general arrangement layouts, equipment datasheets, Operation & Maintenance (O&M) manuals, and calibration schedules. Warranty certificates for the equipment will also be provided, ensuring long-term coverage against any defects.

1. Training and Support: Broaden Energy will deliver a training program for Client staff, covering all aspects of the hydrogen generation system and its operation, maintenance, and safety protocols. The training will include both theoretical and practical components in the UK and our facilities, ensuring that Client personnel are fully trained in the equipment’s use. Training will be scheduled before the final commissioning to ensure readiness, and we will provide support for any further inquiries or technical assistance within 24 hours of receiving a request. 2. Warranty and Maintenance: Broaden Energy will offer a warranty period of 24 months for all equipment. During this period, any defects related to design, manufacturing, or materials will be covered. Maintenance schedules, spare parts inventories, and calibration certificates will be provided during the commissioning phase, ensuring the electrolysers operate at optimal performance throughout its lifecycle. Any necessary software licenses and updates will also be included.

Broaden Energy is a UAE Company, offering a wide range of renewable energy solutions, including solar, wind, and hydrogen generation systems. Providing turnkey EPC solutions for these critical verticals and ensuring sustainable and eco-friendly options tailored to diverse needs.

The hydrogen division of Broaden Energy, known as Broaden Energy Hydrogen Solutions (BEHS), was founded in the UAE as a strategic joint venture between Jiangsu Guofu Hydrogen Equipment and Broaden Energy Solutions to cater to the increasing demand for hydrogen energy solutions. BEHS specializes in the development and manufacturing of hydrogen generation equipment, refueling stations, storage, and transportation systems. We are currently operating from a state-of-the-art manufacturing complex of over 150,000 square meters, capable of producing 2.5 GW of high-efficiency alkaline electrolysers annually while in 2025 our group will increase capacity by adding a manufacturing and maintenance plant of 1GW in the UAE.

Our expertise and manufacturing capability spans the entire hydrogen value chain, ensuring reliable and versatile energy solutions for diverse end-users internationally by having a separate production line for those customers under the JV Broaden Energy Hydrogen Solutions. We also operate and maintain Hydrogen Generation Systems as well as Hydrogen Refueling stations.

Since the Company’s establishment in 2019, Broaden Energy has completed over 800MW of Solar Power Projects in Dubai and Abu Dhabi. In addition, the Company was part of the first Wind Farm Project in the UAE, and most recently build the first Hydrogen Generation and Refueling Station in Abu Dhabi for ADNOC.
Broaden Energy’s hydrogen solutions are among the world’s most efficient and reliable. BEHS has successfully implemented over 150 hydrogen projects worldwide.
2.5 MW Reference project: Hydrogen Generation System with H2 Storage in Ningbo Port, Shanghai

Reference Project: Hydrogen Mobile System Shanghai

Our hydrogen generation systems are designed and built based on a comprehensive set of international and industry-specific standards. These standards ensure that our systems meet the highest levels of safety, efficiency, and environmental compliance. We commit that our equipment and processes are reliable, effective, and capable of achieving the demanding requirements of the hydrogen industry and always put safety first and foremost.
Institutional commitment, training, formalized procedures, management of change, worker participation, lessons learned.

To ensure Broaden Energy delivers both the flexibility of modular deployment and the cost efficiencies of large-scale processing, we have developed and propose two fully compliant configurations. The

consists of seven 5 MW electrolyser stacks in accordance to the RFP, where each BEHS 1000 AWE containerized module is deployed independently from another for phased commissioning and maximum operational redundancy. The

comprises two 40 MW blocks each a BEHS 4000 (20 MW modules) designed to consolidate separation and purification, reducing instrumentation counts and leveraging economies of scale. The below table compares the options.
layout and render of 7 Nos of BEHS-1000
layout  and render of 2 Nos of BEHS-4000

The core component of the hydrogen generation system is the BEHS high efficiency alkaline electrolyser, used to produce hydrogen and oxygen through the process of electrolysis of water. To ensure that the produced hydrogen meets the project requirements, the system is equipped with a lye gas separation and purification system to remove water, alkaline solution, and trace gases such as oxygen from the hydrogen product, ensuring the purity of the hydrogen. The system is supported by various utility systems such as the water purification system that purifies tap water into demineralized water ready to be mixed with a dosage of Hydrogen Peroxide flake. The system also integrates an Energy Management System (EMS), enabling the electrolyser to adapt to various power sources such as the dynamic load fluctuations generated during photovoltaic (PV) power generation. The EMS can adjust the electrolyser load in real-time based on the power fluctuations from the photovoltaic field output, ensuring that the hydrogen production process meets project requirements even under unstable green electricity supply conditions.

To ensure the stable operation of the hydrogen production system, the project is equipped with a comprehensive set of utilities systems, including a purified water system, cooling system, power supply system, instrument air supply system, and control and monitoring systems. The purified water system supplies high-purity water to the electrolyser, and it includes a purified water unit, water storage tanks, and purified water pumps to ensure the purity and stable supply of water during the hydrogen production process. The power supply system consists of switching equipment, rectifiers, and rectifier transformers, ensuring a stable power supply for the system's operation. The cooling water and chilled water circulation systems, combined with heat exchangers, are used to control the temperature of the system, ensuring the equipment operates under optimal conditions. Other utilities facilities include pipelines and connecting devices, control systems, lighting, CCTV systems, lightning protection and grounding systems, operational pathways, wastewater collection pond, nitrogen cylinder racks, and instrument air supply systems. These systems collectively ensure the stable operation and safety of the hydrogen production system.

In terms of safety, the hydrogen production system is equipped with hydrogen analysers, hydrogen leak detectors, and flame detectors. These devices can trigger emergency shutdowns in the event of a hydrogen leak or other emergency situations, preventing any potential hazards to the plant. An independent Safety Instrumented System (SIS) is responsible for the safety monitoring of the hydrogen production system, ensuring automatic safety measures are taken in emergency situations. In addition, the system is equipped with real-time monitoring and data logging software and hardware, enabling continuous monitoring and recording of system operation, ensuring comprehensive data collection. The system also includes an alkaline solution conductivity detector to promptly identify anomalies in the electrolyser operation, thereby enhancing the operational stability of the system. Furthermore, the electrolyser cells voltage monitoring equipment improves the precision of monitoring the electrolyser operating conditions. Independent alkaline supply and recovery systems are provided to facilitate the handling of alkaline solution during commissioning startup and shutdown phases. The alkaline solution circulation pumps and makeup water pumps are configured in a one-operating, one-standby mode, effectively reducing the impact of equipment failures on system operation.

The hydrogen production system containers and equipment are equipped with insulation to maintain the temperature of the alkaline solution for extended periods, minimizing restart time and energy consumption. This insulation also ensures safe and stable operation under various conditions. The equipment is supplied in a containerized format, with a high level of integration and a compact layout, effectively saving space and reducing civil construction costs. The system is also designed with external protective features such as dust, hail, and wind resistance to withstand the harsh desert conditions. Additionally, an air-conditioned control room is provided to ensure the equipment operates smoothly in extreme environments while offering a comfortable workspace for operators.
The hydrogen produced by the BEHS electrolyser is routed into our fully integrated
Storage & Compression Package Part B and Part C

: Low-pressure hydrogen is first pressurized by eight (8) 45 MPa, oil-free, hydraulically driven compressors, each rated at 1 000 Nm³/h @ 1.4 MPa inlet. A second stage of two (2) 70 MPa compressors further boosts pressure for high-pressure dispensing and cylinder filling. All compressors feature real-time leak and oil-contamination monitoring, and are supported by dedicated air-cooled chillers to maintain discharge temperatures ≤ 40 °C.

: To smooth transient flows and decouple production from dispensing, the system includes either seven (7) × 5 m³ or two (2) × 20 m³ buffer tanks at 1.4 MPa. Bulk storage is provided by nine (9) sets of 45 MPa cylinder banks (~ 173 kg H₂ each) and one (1) set of 90 MPa cylinders (~ 93.5 kg H₂), all PED-compliant and monitored via pressure and temperature sensors . We also included a dispensser for 350 and 700 Bar refueling capability integrated with the one set of 90Mpa storage able to refuell the JCB Equipment dispensing of at least 100 kg per day (dependant on schedule of production).

: A PLC-based sequence control panel orchestrates compressor staging, buffer charging, and cylinder switching to optimize dispenser availability and minimize pressure swings. A DN40 venting tower safely handles overpressure events. Nitrogen purge racks and an instrument-air supply system ensure clean, dry actuation of all process valves.

: Dedicated chiller units for each compressor stage and dispenser maintain optimal operating temperatures (5–15 °C for compressors; −45–−40 °C for dispensers). Insulated piping and containerized skids preserve gas temperature and reduce energy losses.

: A comprehensive PLC/SCADA setup manages compression, storage, and dispensing sequences, interlocks on hydrogen analyzers, leak and flame detectors, and emergency shutdown logic. Real-time data logging and remote monitoring interfaces provide complete operational visibility and ensure rapid response to any anomaly ​
Render of plant and Scope 1 Scope Of Supply
Based on Figure 1 showing the Scope of Supply, the project's boundaries are as follows:
The project boundary primarily encompasses the hydrogen production system and its associated utility systems, which include:

Station Control System and Energy Management System (EMS): Responsible for managing the power inputs, ensuring the stable operation of the hydrogen production system under dynamic and grid load conditions. The EMS adjusts the electrolyser’s load distribution in real-time based on the output power and predicted output power information provided by a renewable energy system, as well as power supply from the grid, ensuring the system runs efficiently and safely.

Rectifiers/Transformers (R/T): These components convert the power managed by the EMS into the appropriate voltage and current for use by the electrolysers, ensuring the system operates safely and stably.
Electrolysers: The core equipment, responsible for generating hydrogen (H₂) and oxygen (O₂) through water electrolysis
Separation System: Carries out the preliminary separation of the hydrogen produced, yielding unpurified hydrogen
Purification System: Further purifies the crude hydrogen, ensuring it meets the required quality standards
Utility Systems: Includes supporting facilities essential for the hydrogen production system operation:
Compressed Air System: Provides compressed air for equipment operation.
Nitrogen Supply System: Supplies nitrogen for purging or inerting to prevent unwanted reactions.
Pure Water System: Delivers high-purity water for the electrolyser’s electrolysis process.
Chilled Water System: Manages system temperatures to ensure optimal equipment performance.
The qualified hydrogen & Oxygen is transported through pipelines to buffer tanks and compression.
Eight (8) × 45 MPa and two (2) × 70 MPa oil-free, hydraulically driven units with integrated chillers and real-time leak detection.
Under Main Offer (Seven (7) × 5 m³) or under Alternative (Two (2) × 20 m³) at 1.4-1.6 MPa to smooth production–dispense fluctuations.
Nine (9) sets at 45 MPa (~ 173 kg H₂ each) plus one (1) set at 90 MPa (~ 93.5 kg H₂).
PLC-based logic for compressor staging, buffer charging, cylinder switching; DN40 vent with flame arrestor.
Two (2) × 70 MPa dispensers (TK17 nozzles) and eight (8) × 35 MPa filling columns; metering, flow/pressure sensors, emergency-shutdown interlocks, and integrated chiller for pre-cooling.
The sections outside the project boundary include the input resources needed for the hydrogen production system's operation and the output hydrogen, specifically:
Grid: The grid serves as a main power source, providing stable power for the plant
Reverse Osmosis: RO water is supplied to the hydrogen production system, where it is processed by the pure water system to produce high-purity water for the electrolyser’s electrolysis process.

Hydrogen production by water electrolysis is a process of decomposing electrolyte dissolved in water into new substances by means of direct current. However, when direct current is introduced into some electrolyte aqueous solutions, the decomposed substances have nothing associated with the original electrolyte. What is decomposed is pure water as the solvent, and the original electrolyte, such as sulfuric acid, sodium hydroxide and potassium hydroxide, remains in the water.

In the electrolysis of water, as pure water is a typical weak electrolyte because of its low ionization and low conductivity, it is necessary to add the aforementioned electrolytes to increase the conductivity of the solution, so that pure water can be electrolysed into hydrogen and oxygen.

In the past, sodium hydroxide was often used as electrolyte in China, but as the material of electrolysis stack can resist the corrosion of potassium hydroxide, potassium hydroxide solution has been commonly used as electrolyte to save electric energy. Electrolytes such as potassium hydroxide will not be electrolysed. Taking potassium hydroxide as an example, it is a strong electrolyte, and when dissolved in water, the ionization process occurs as follows:

Therefore, a large amount of K+ and OH- are produced in aqueous solution. Metal ions have different activity in aqueous solution, and have a decreasing activity as follows: K>Na>Mg>Al>Mn>Zn>Fe>Ni>Sn>Pb>H>Cu>Hg>Ag>Au

Pure water is a weak electrolyte and is difficult to electrolyze. However, when KOH is dissolved in water, polar water molecules surround ionized K+ and become hydrated potassium ions, and the water molecules get polar orientations due to the action of K+. Under the action of direct current, K+ moves to the cathode together with the water molecules with polar orientations, and then H+ will get electrons first and become hydrogen. When the electrolytic equation of water acts on potassium hydroxide aqueous solution under the action of direct current, the following discharge reactions occur at the cathode and anode, as illustrated below:
The process of water electrolysis into hydrogen and oxygen and its electrode reactions are as follows:
The H+ (produced by ionization of water) in the electrolyte is attracted by and moves to the cathode where it receives electrons to form hydrogen.
The OH- in the electrolyte is attracted by and moves to the anode and finally releases electrons to form water and oxygen.
In the electrolysis process where KOH is the electrolyte, water is actually electrolyzed to produce hydrogen and oxygen, while KOH only plays the role of carrying charge.
The theoretical water consumption for producing 1m 3 hydrogen is 0.804kg. The actual water consumption is higher than the theoretical one because both hydrogen and oxygen carry away some water.
As a result, the actual water consumption for producing 1m 3 of hydrogen is about 0.85kg to 0.9kg.

A water electrolysis cell is composed of a pair of electrodes immersed in the electrolyte and a diaphragm spaced between them to prevent gas penetration. Water will be electrolyzed by direct current at a certain voltage (above the decomposition voltage of water, 1.23V, and the thermal balance voltage, 1.47V). According to the output demand of user, several groups of electrolysis cells are combined to reduce the volume and increase the output, thus forming a pressure filtration combined structure of electrolysis stack. Generally, a medium-pressure electrolysis stack adopts a parallel structure of the left and right stacks, with the middle pole plate connected to positive DC power supply and both end pole plates connected to negative DC power supply. It uses double polarity pole plates and diaphragm spacers to form multiple electrolysis cells, and it has common inlet and outlet in the lower part of the stack, and respective gas and liquid channels for hydrogen and oxygen bases in the upper part. A tandem-type structure with one positive and two negatives or one positive and one negative is used, and its internal structure is similar to the coupled-type structure.

The proposed operating pressure of the electrolysis stack, hydrogen production system and hydrogen purification system of this project is 1.4MPa. DC consumption efficiency is 4.3±0.1 kWh/Nm 3 H 2 . The alkaline electrolysis stack is featured by large hydrogen production, high hydrogen purity, long service life of equipment, reliable operation and low requirements for operation and maintenance, and can be combined with solar energy to meet the requirements of large-duty green hydrogen production. The hydrogen produced can be applied to chemical, steel, fuel cell and other scenarios with high purity hydrogen demand, while the oxygen produced can be recycled or directly discharged.
Technical Features of the BEHS-1000 AWE

1. Under a cold start at a tank temperature of 15°C, the current and voltage can achieve dynamic response in seconds. 2. Within 3 minutes of a hot start, the current can increase to the rated working current, achieving stable hydrogen production. 3. As the temperature increases, the voltage gradually decreases under constant current mode, thus reducing direct current consumption. When the temperature exceeds 75°C, the direct current consumption is 4.2±0.1 kWh/Nm³ H₂. 4. After reaching the rated state, the electrolyser exhibits minimal fluctuations, operating stably, safely, and reliably. 5. The operating temperature of the electrolyser must be maintained between 85-92°C.

1. Hydrogen Separation Principle: The primary role of the hydrogen separation system is to separate the hydrogen produced by the electrolyser from the electrolyte, achieving preliminary purification of hydrogen and oxygen. Under the action of direct current, the potassium hydroxide solution in the electrolyser is decomposed into hydrogen and oxygen. This mixture is then sent to the hydrogen and oxygen liquid separator for separation. The two gases are further cooled and dehydrated by the hydrogen and oxygen coolers; oxygen is directly discharged, while hydrogen is sent to the purification device for further deoxidization and drying. The electrolyte separated by the hydrogen-oxygen separator is cooled and filtered by the alkaline solution circulation pump before returning to the electrolyser for continued electrolysis. The water supply device automatically replenishes water based on the liquid level in the hydrogen and oxygen liquid separator.
Main Structural Components of the Separation System
The hydrogen and alkaline solution mixture produced by the electrolyser is separated, washed, and cooled in this separation system, which mainly consists of the following components:

1. Hydrogen Separator and Oxygen Separator: 2. Function: Separates the hydrogen (oxygen) and alkaline solution mixture from the electrolyser 3. Material: Stainless steel 4. Structure: The hydrogen and oxygen separation units are horizontal tanks equipped with a level gauge and have gas-liquid inlets, gas outlets, alkaline balance interfaces, and alkaline solution outlets

1. Hydrogen Scrubber and Oxygen Scrubber: 2. Function: Washes the separated hydrogen (oxygen) to remove residual alkaline solution from the gas 3. Material: Stainless steel 4. Structure: The washers are vertical tanks with gas inlets, gas outlets, water supply inlets, and overflow outlets 2. Hydrogen Cooler and Oxygen Cooler: 6. Function: Cools the washed hydrogen (oxygen) to remove most of the moisture from the gas 7. Material: Stainless steel 8. Structure: The containers are equipped with gas inlets, gas outlets, and overflow outlets 3. Alkaline Solution Filter: 10. Function: Removes impurities and debris from the alkaline solution entering the electrolyser 11. Material: Stainless steel 12. Structure: Has inlets and outlets at both ends, with a removable filter screen end cover in the middle 4. Alkaline Solution Circulation Pump (Shielded Pump): 14. Function: Provides power for the circulation of the alkaline solution 5. Gas-Water Separator: 16. Function: Separates gas and water after the hydrogen (oxygen) exits the cooler 17. Material: Stainless steel 18. Structure: Equipped with gas inlets, gas outlets, and drainage outlets 6. Collection Tank: 20. Function: Acts as a secondary buffer tank for the discharge from the gas-liquid separator 21. Material: Stainless steel 22. Structure: Contains gas-liquid inlets and outlets
Table 2-4 Single Set Separation System Parameter Table

1. Hydrogen Purification Principle: The hydrogen purification system consists of a deoxidizer, cooler, gas-water separator, hydrogen drying towers A and B, pre-drying tower C, liquid collection tank, valves, piping, fittings, primary instruments, and a frame. The purification control cabinet is the core of the control section, enabling automatic adjustment, display, alarm, interlocking, and other functions.
Main Structural Components of the Purification System
The purification system mainly removes oxygen, dehydrates, and filters dust from the hydrogen produced by the separation system.

1. Hydrogen Deoxidizer: 2. Function: Removes oxygen from the hydrogen 3. Material: Stainless steel 2. Hydrogen Dryer: 5. Function: Removes water from the hydrogen 6. Material: Stainless steel 3. Hydrogen Cooler: 8. Function: Cools the moisture removed from the hydrogen in the drying tower 9. Material: Stainless steel 4. Gas-Water Separator: 11. Function: Separates gas and liquid from the cooled hydrogen. 12. Material: Stainless steel 5. Liquid Collection Tank: 14. Function: Acts as a secondary buffer tank for the discharge from the gas-water separator 15. Material: Stainless steel 6. Hydrogen Filter: 17. Function: Removes solid particles and other impurities from the hydrogen 18. Material: Stainless steel
Table 2-5: Single Set Purification System Parameter Table

Hydrogen Deoxidation System : This system uses catalytic deoxidation, cooling and dehumidification, and adsorption drying methods to remove impurities and purify hydrogen. Raw hydrogen enters the deoxidizer, which is filled with room-temperature catalytic deoxidizer. A small amount of oxygen in the hydrogen reacts with the catalyst to form water, reducing the oxygen content to below 5 ppm. An electric heating element is installed in the deoxidizer to raise the temperature, allowing the water generated by the reaction to be removed in gaseous form.

Hydrogen Drying and Regeneration System: The dryer is equipped with an absorbent that has a large adsorption capacity and good temperature resistance. Three dryers work alternately for regeneration and adsorption, ensuring the continuity of the entire system. The working principle of the three-tower drying process is detailed below:
In one switching cycle, the dryer experiences three states:
Z1: A auxiliary, B adsorption, C regeneration;
Z2: A regeneration, B auxiliary, C adsorption;
Z3: A working, B regeneration, C auxiliary.

Note: In each state, the raw hydrogen enters the first dryer after deoxidation, cooling, and gas-water separation in the working state. The processing gas volume is the full gas volume, with no heating elements working in the dryer, and the medium is undehydrated raw hydrogen. The second dryer is in regeneration mode, with processing gas volume determined during commissioning (could be the full volume or part of it).
Regeneration mode includes a heating stage and a cooling stage;
Heating stage: The heating element in the dryer operates, and when the upper temperature reaches the interlocking value or the heating time reaches the set value, heating automatically stops;
Cooling stage: After stopping heating, the airflow continues to flow through the dryer, cooling it until the dryer switches back to the working state;
In the regeneration phase, the medium is dehydrated dry hydrogen;
The third dryer is in adsorption mode, processing the same gas volume as the second dryer, with no heating elements working, and the medium is regenerated hydrogen.
Each layer has distinct roles and responsibilities, as described below:

This layer consists of the Upper Computer, which is responsible for system-wide monitoring and data visualization. It provides operators with real-time operational status, historical data, and alarm information. The Supervisory Layer does not perform direct control actions but serves as the interface for system monitoring and information display.

The Control Layer is the core of the system operational control. It primarily utilizes the Energy Management System (EMS) and Programmable Logic Controllers (PLCs) to execute control commands and manage equipment operations. The structure is as follows:

EMS (Energy Management System): The EMS collects real-time data from the system, optimizes system operation, and issues control commands. It ensures the balance between photovoltaic power output and hydrogen production load.

Process PLC: The Process PLC receives control commands from the EMS and regulates the hydrogen production load by controlling the PCS Cabinet (Power Conversion System), which adjusts the operational status of the electrolyser.

Safety PLC: The Safety PLC is responsible for safety protection functions, ensuring safe operation of the system. It works with the SIS Cabinet (Safety Instrumented System) to monitor and control critical system parameters, preventing hazardous conditions.

The Field Layer comprises physical equipment, including photovoltaic generation units, electrolysers, and other hydrogen production devices. This layer is responsible for executing actual operations and communicates with the control system through sensors and actuators. It provides real-time feedback on equipment status (e.g., temperature, pressure) and performs tasks based on commands from the PLCs.
The on-site instrument equipment is explosion-proof, with an explosion-proof rating of not less than IICT4 and a protection rating of IP65.
All electrical equipment within the hydrogen production system meets the explosion-proof requirements for Zone 2.

Low-voltage motors should be high-efficiency motors; motors must have an efficiency of at least IE4 as per IEC60034-30. High-voltage motors should also be energy-efficient (no efficiency requirements for variable frequency motors).
Motor insulation should be rated at F, with a temperature rise assessed at B. The protection rating for the motor and junction box should both be IP65.

The water electrolysis hydrogen production device utilizes a PLC automatic control system to automatically adjust the main parameters of the device: pressure, temperature, and hydrogen-oxygen liquid level difference. The operating parameters of the device are displayed centrally. In case of deviations from the set values for post-pressure of the pressure regulating valve, cooling water pressure, gas source pressure, upper and lower limits of hydrogen-oxygen levels, and hydrogen-oxygen purity, an automatic alarm will be triggered. The control system ensures the display of process parameters during startup, operation, and fault conditions, while also ensuring the normal startup, safe operation, and fault alarm functionality of all equipment in the system, achieving automatic control and interlocking functionality, with data sharing.

PLC automatic control includes but is not limited to data collection, processing, and interlocking control of important system parameters. The entire system has functions for automatic maintenance of normal operation and automatic alarms. The contractor is responsible for debugging the hardware, logic, and screen configuration of the PLC system.

The control system should have a complete alarm interlocking function. All parameters related to system safe operation must have alarm and interlocking functions set, and their upper and lower limit values can be adjusted online according to user needs. If the system exceeds the interlocking limits, it should be able to automatically cut off the power supply. The entire system should also have phased safety release measures.
The PLC control cabinet should reserve 10% of spare control interfaces for both analog and digital signals.
The explosion-proof rating of the supplied instruments strictly adheres to relevant standard requirements.

The core components of the automatic control system mainly include a programmable logic controller (PLC), electrical converters, and control cabinets to ensure the safe, stable, and reliable operation of the entire system.

The entire system adopts PLC control to automatically adjust the main parameters of the device: pressure, temperature, and hydrogen-oxygen liquid level difference. The working pressure, temperature, hydrogen liquid level, oxygen liquid level, hydrogen purity, and oxygen purity can be displayed centrally. If there are deviations in post-pressure of the hydrogen valve, gas source pressure, upper and lower limits of hydrogen-oxygen liquid levels, and hydrogen-oxygen purity, an automatic audio-visual alarm will be triggered. If the primary parameters of the device, such as pressure, temperature, and hydrogen-oxygen liquid levels, deviate too far from normal values without timely processing, the device will automatically trigger an audio-visual alarm and stop operation.

The control system consists of an upper-level data management machine and a Siemens programmable logic controller (PLC). The operational data and parameters of the entire system are collected, processed, and transmitted to the local upper-level data management machine via a network using PLC modules installed in the control cabinet, thus completing the management of the operational data of the entire system.
The control cabinet is the core part of the automatic control system, responsible for the safe, stable, and reliable operation of the entire system. It can be divided into the following components:

Programmable Logic Controller (PLC): The PLC is the core device that performs automatic control functions, regulating the working pressure of the device, controlling the operating temperature, balancing the hydrogen-oxygen liquid levels, and handling alarms and interlocks.
Control Detection Instruments: Mainly includes oxygen analyzers in hydrogen, hydrogen analyzers in oxygen, dew point instruments, and trace oxygen analyzers.

Isolation Barriers: Due to the hydrogen environment of the hydrogen production and purification devices, certain explosion-proof requirements are needed. All electrical signals from instruments and meters located in hazardous areas entering the control cabinet first pass through isolation barriers before entering the cabinet, ensuring compliance with relevant explosion-proof requirements.
Solenoid Valves: Used to drive the field pneumatic ball valves.

Air Filter and Pressure Regulator: There are two types of air filter and pressure regulators, primarily used to filter and regulate the pressure of the instrument gas source. One path reduces the gas source pressure to 0.2 MPa to provide qualified gas source for the three electrical converters; the other path reduces the gas source pressure to 0.4-0.6 MPa to provide qualified gas source for the pneumatic ball valves.
Electrical Converters: The standard 4-20 mA signals output from the PLC
Safety Instrumented System (SIS) Overview and Design Principles

The Safety Instrumented System (SIS) is an independent safety protection system separate from the Process Control System (PCS) that reduces risks in production processes. It monitors critical process parameters and takes immediate action, such as triggering emergency shutdowns or activating safety devices, when parameters exceed pre-set safety limits. This helps prevent accidents or mitigate their consequences.

1. Design Principles 2. Independence: The SIS should operate independently from the Basic Process Control System (BPCS) to ensure that even in the event of a BPCS failure, the SIS can reliably execute its safety functions. 3. Reliability: The system should employ highly reliable hardware and software components to ensure accurate and timely responses when needed. 4. Safety: The design and operation of the system must comply with relevant safety standards and regulations to meet all necessary safety requirements. 5. Maintainability: The system should be easy to maintain and test, allowing potential faults to be identified and repaired promptly. 2. System Components 7. Sensors: Sensors monitor key process parameters such as temperature, pressure, flow, and liquid levels. These sensors must be highly reliable and accurate. 8. Logic Controllers: Based on input signals from the sensors, logic controllers make decisions according to pre-defined logic, determining whether safety actions need to be triggered. 9. Actuators: These execute the necessary safety actions based on signals from the logic controllers, such as closing valves, stopping motors, or activating safety devices. The actuators must provide quick and reliable response times. 3. Functional Description: 11. Monitoring Function: 12. Real-time Data Collection: The SIS is equipped with high-precision sensors that continuously and in real-time collect critical process data, including but not limited to temperature, pressure, flow, liquid levels, and concentrations. The sensors transmit the collected data to the logic controllers as electrical signals. 13. Data Processing and Analysis: The logic controllers process and analyze the sensor data. They can perform tasks such as filtering, amplifying, and converting data to improve accuracy and reliability. Additionally, the controllers can analyze the data using pre-set algorithms to assess whether the process is in a normal state. 14. Anomaly Detection and Alarming: When monitored parameters exceed the pre-set safety limits, the SIS immediately issues an alarm. This can be communicated through visual and audible signals, as well as on-screen prompts to alert operators. Additionally, alarm information can be sent to relevant management departments or emergency response centers to facilitate timely action. 15. Logic Decision-Making Function: 16. Safety Logic Design: Safety logic is designed based on the characteristics of the process and safety requirements. Typically, Boolean logic, sequential logic, or other forms of logic are used to determine whether safety risks exist. For example, if both the temperature and pressure exceed certain thresholds, a safety action may be triggered. 17. Logical Operations and Decision-Making: The logic controllers perform logical operations (such as AND, OR, NOT) on the collected data based on the safety logic to determine whether a safety action needs to be triggered. During decision-making, the system can also take into account other factors, such as equipment status or operational mode. 18. Safety Action Execution Function: 19. Actuator Control: When logic determines that a safety action is required, the SIS immediately activates the actuators to carry out the corresponding safety action. Actuators can include valves, motors, pumps, and other equipment that are controlled via electrical, pneumatic, or hydraulic signals. 20. Types of Safety Actions: The type of safety actions depends on the process and safety requirements. Common safety actions include emergency shutdowns, closing valves, and cutting off power supplies. These actions can effectively prevent or mitigate accidents. 21. Response Time: SIS safety actions must have a rapid response time to ensure that preventative measures are taken before an accident occurs. The response time is typically required to be within milliseconds to tens of milliseconds, depending on the process hazards and safety requirements. 22. Fault Diagnosis Function: 23. System Self-Diagnosis: The SIS has self-diagnosis capabilities, enabling it to continuously monitor its own hardware and software. It can detect sensor failures, logic controller faults, actuator malfunctions, etc., and issue warnings when necessary. The system also records fault information for subsequent analysis and troubleshooting. 24. Fault Isolation and Recovery: When a fault is detected, the SIS can automatically isolate the faulty section to ensure the normal operation of other parts of the system. The system can also attempt to automatically restore the faulty section or prompt operators to manually restore it. During fault recovery, the system ensures that safety functions remain unaffected. 25. Remote Diagnosis and Maintenance: To improve maintainability, the SIS can support remote diagnostics and maintenance. Through network connections, maintenance personnel can remotely monitor the system status, diagnose faults, and perform software updates or parameter adjustments, significantly reducing maintenance costs and downtime. 26. Fault History and Analysis: The SIS records a detailed history of all faults, including the time, type, location, and corrective actions taken. This information provides valuable insights for future fault analysis and troubleshooting. Analyzing historical fault data allows maintenance personnel to identify potential issues or weak points in the system, enabling preventive measures to improve reliability. Furthermore, fault history can be used to evaluate system performance and maintenance effectiveness, providing data to support system optimization and improvement.

The hydrogen production facility can use various sources of energy, for grid or certified power or renewable energy. To facilitate different load requirements we have developed a proprietary Energy Management System to extend green hydrogen production while connected to intermittent sources and reliably operate under the grid when connected to non intermittent energy source

The electrical network architecture proposed for this system will utilize an AC network approach, connecting the plant to AC bus bars (33 kV and 11/0.4 kV) from the grid. The medium-voltage AC bus will step down the power to supply the electrolysesr, while the 11kV voltage AC bus will provide power to the Balance of Plant (BOP) systems and auxiliary utilities at the station. Figure 2.18a describes the flow diagram of EMS.
Figure 2.1.8a Block Flow Diagram of EMS

1. Power Flow: Power flows from the photovoltaic (PV) system and the grid through the medium-voltage distribution board (MV DB) and low-voltage distribution board (LV DB) to the electrolyser and utilities systems. 2. Communication Lines: Using communication protocols like Mod-bus TCP and RS485, the EMS collects power data from the PV system and transmits control signals to the relevant equipment through the PLC. 3. Power Conditions: During hydrogen production, the formula  indicates that under specific hydrogen production conditions, the power drawn from the grid should be less than the PV output, ensuring that renewable energy is prioritized.
The compressor package converts low‐pressure hydrogen from the electrolyser into high‐pressure gas suitable for storage and dispensing. It comprises:

Eight (8) 45 MPa compressors (1 000 Nm³/h @ 1.4 MPa inlet) and two (2) 70 MPa booster compressors (668 Nm³/h @ 30–50 MPa inlet) are skid-mounted with integrated hydraulic power units and piston-seal cylinders. The oil-free design eliminates gas contamination, and a special packing arrangement prevents hydraulic-oil ingress into the hydrogen stream. Real-time gas-and-oil leak detection ensures purity and safety .

Each compressor train is housed in a weatherproof, insulated container (IP55) with vibration isolation mounts and acoustic enclosures. Noise emissions are limited to < 80 dB(A) at 1 m in compliance with BLCH PSR1 Basic of Design requirements

Dedicated air-cooled chillers maintain discharge temperatures ≤ 40 °C for 45 MPa compressors and 5–15 °C for 70 MPa compressors. All chiller units feature stainless-steel coolant tanks, closed-loop circuits, and operate on R410a refrigerant

Local PLC cabinets with 10″ HMI panels provide compressor start/stop, load sequencing, and safety interlocks. Key process variables (pressure, temperature, vibration, oil level) are monitored continuously, and emergency shutdowns are initiated on alarm conditions. The compressor control system integrates with the central EMS for coordinated plant operation

All compressors conform to ATEX 2014/34/EU, Pressure Equipment (Safety) Regulations 2016, and relevant ISO/EN standards. Factory Acceptance Tests (FAT) and Site Acceptance Tests (SAT) will follow BLCH-SPEC-COM-009 “Compressor Schedule 3 – Performance Guarantees and Testing,” ensuring guaranteed performance levels without manual overrides or bypass of safety interlocks
Bradford Low Carbon Hydrogen –Compressor and Booster Compressor Datasheets

Compression of hydrogen gas from a suction pressure between 300 barg and 500 barg, up to a discharge pressure above 700 barg, to dispense gas directly into SSPs. Upstream of the suction side will be static storage which holds up to 500 barg and can deplete down to 300 barg. The required settle pressure inside the bowsers is 700 barg.
Booster compressor to cover 60 kg/h (≈ 668 Nm³/h) flow (per compressor). Normal conditions defined as 0 °C, 1 atm.
The Supply of Machinery (Safety) Regulations 2008; Pressure Equipment (Safety) Regulations 2016; ATEX 2014/34/EU; The Electromagnetic Compatibility Regulations 2016

• Water volume per set: 6 m³ (1 m³ per unit) • Operating temperature: –40 / 60 °C • Design temperature: –40 / 60 °C • Operating pressure: 45 MPa • Design pressure: 50 MPa • Storage capacity per set: ~173 kg H₂ • Total capacity: ~1 559 kg H₂

• Water volume per set: 2 m³ (0.5 m³ per unit) • Operating temperature: –40 / 60 °C • Design temperature: –40 / 60 °C • Operating pressure: 90 MPa • Design pressure: 99 MPa • Storage capacity per set: 93.5 kg H₂

To provide short‐term buffering and ensure uninterrupted refuelling operations—even under variable production or dispensing rates—we have proposed the storage system to incorporate high‐pressure cylinder banks designed for modular, skid‐mounted installation:
Nine 45 MPa cylinder sets (6 m³ water volume each) deliver ~1 559 kg of hydrogen capacity, smoothing the difference between electrolyser output and dispenser demand.
One 90 MPa cylinder set (2 m³ water volume) serves as a high‐pressure reserve, enabling rapid fills and maintaining dispenser pressure during peak demand.
Cylinder banks are mounted on galvanized‐steel skids with integrated manifolds, quick‐disconnects, and stainless‐steel (316L) piping per ASME B31.3.
Each manifold includes fill/discharge valves, back‐pressure regulators, and pressure relief devices (PRVs and bursting discs) to safeguard against overpressure.
Pressure transducers and thermocouples monitor each set’s status in real time; signals feed into the central SCADA/PLC for automated refill sequencing, leak detection, and emergency shutdown.
A local HMI panel on the skid allows manual intervention and diagnostics during commissioning or maintenance.
Overpressure venting is routed through a flame‐arrested DN40 vent line to a safe outdoor location.
Manual and automatic nitrogen‐purge connections permit inerting of the manifold during cylinder exchange or maintenance.
Skids are rated for outdoor use (IP55), with weatherproof coatings, seismic anchoring provisions, and insulation jackets to limit thermal losses in temperature extremes (–40 / +60 °C).
The compact footprint of the cylinder skids minimizes civil works and simplifies site layout, enabling rapid “plug‐and‐play” installation alongside the compressor and dispenser modules.

The dispenser package provides the final link between high-pressure storage and vehicle refuelling. It is designed for fast, accurate, and safe delivery of hydrogen at both 35 MPa and 70 MPa, with fully integrated temperature control, metering, and safety interlocks.
35Mpa / 70 MPa Dispensers (TK16/17 Nozzles): Two skid-mounted units, each capable of delivering up to 3.6 kg/min with integrated alcohol pre-cooling to –40 °C. Nozzle supply power 0.3 kW.

35 MPa Filling Columns: Eight fixed-column dispensers with cascade pressure control, flowmeters (±1 % accuracy), and automatic residual-pressure compensation to maximize fill speed, one filling column is dual purpose and can serve the vehicle dispenser.
Cryogenic pre-cooling loop (R-134a) reduces gas temperature to –40 °C before dispensing, preventing adiabatic heating and ensuring correct density measurement.
Dedicated chiller units (700 bar service) maintain dispenser and nozzle temperatures within –45 to –40 °C.
Local PLC panels with 10″ HMI display real-time flow, pressure, and temperature.
Mass flow meters, pressure transducers, and differential-pressure switches feed into the station EMS for automated fill sequencing and stock management.
Emergency-stop buttons and dispenser nozzle interlocks comply with EN ISO 17268 and SAE J2601 standards.
Integrated hydrogen-leak detectors at each nozzle.
Over-pressure relief via flame-arrested DN40 vent lines.
Automatic purge with nitrogen when dispenser is idle or during maintenance.
CE-marked to ATEX 2014/34/EU, PED 2014/68/EU, and EMC 2014/30/EU.
Factory Acceptance Tests include: flow-rate verification, pre-cooling performance, leak-tightness, emergency shutdown response, and metering accuracy.
35 MPa operating; 50 MPa design; mass-flow metering

Broaden Energy will provide the necessary support to the Client (Supervision only). The Client and Broaden, will work closely together who will monitor the installation works, to maintain the highest standards.

We confirm that we will provide training to Client’s technicians at the project site in Bradford UK, and in our facilities in the UAE. The training will cover an introduction to the equipment design and specifications, operation and maintenance procedures, software training, and other aspects relevant to enabling the client staff to operate, maintain, and calibrate the supplied equipment. The training will consist of both theoretical and practical sessions, and detailed training manuals will be provided in English. These manuals will align with the course material and offer supplementary information to aid in understanding the operation and maintenance manuals. Hands-on training will be conducted using installed equipment or spares, which will be returned to inventory in suitable condition after use. On-site training will also be provided during the installation and commissioning phases. Any chemicals, raw materials, or other necessary items for practical training will be supplied by BEHS for the initial training.
Prior to take-over, Broaden Energy will supply the required documentation in compliance with the project requirements, including:
Detailed schematic drawings of equipment installation
Comprehensive operation and maintenance manuals, including consignment inventory or on-site spare parts recommendations
All documentation will be provided in the English Language.
